🔬 Science

Matter

Explore the building blocks of everything around us - matter!

📚 Unit 6 ⏱️ 8 min read

Unit 6: Matter

Matter is anything that has mass and takes up space. Everything around you – air, water, food, books, and even your body – is made of matter.

What is Matter?

PropertyMeaningExample
MassHow much matter is in an objectA brick has more mass than a feather
VolumeHow much space an object takes upA balloon takes up space

💡 Things that are NOT matter: Light, heat, sound, thoughts. These have no mass and do not take up space.

States of Matter

Matter exists in three main states:

StateShapeVolumeParticle arrangement
SolidFixed shapeFixed volumeParticles are tightly packed
LiquidTakes shape of containerFixed volumeParticles can slide past each other
GasFills any containerFills any spaceParticles are far apart

Properties of Solids

PropertyExplanation
Definite shapeDoes not change shape on its own
Definite volumeTakes up a fixed amount of space
Cannot flowStays where it is placed
Cannot be compressedCannot be squeezed into a smaller space

Examples of solids: Wood, stone, ice, iron, plastic, book

Properties of Liquids

PropertyExplanation
No definite shapeTakes the shape of its container
Definite volumeTakes up a fixed amount of space
Can flowPours and moves
Can be slightly compressedVery hard to squeeze

Examples of liquids: Water, milk, oil, juice, honey

Properties of Gases

PropertyExplanation
No definite shapeFills the entire container
No definite volumeExpands to fill any space
Can flowMoves freely
Can be compressedCan be squeezed into smaller space

Examples of gases: Air, oxygen, carbon dioxide, steam

Comparison Table: States of Matter

PropertySolidLiquidGas
ShapeFixedTakes container shapeFills container
VolumeFixedFixedFills container
FlowNoYesYes
CompressibleNoAlmost noYes
Particle movementVibrate in placeSlide past each otherMove freely
ExampleIceWaterSteam

Changes of State

Matter can change from one state to another when heat is added or removed.

The Six Changes of State

ChangeFrom → ToHowExample
MeltingSolid → LiquidAdd heatIce melts into water
FreezingLiquid → SolidRemove heatWater freezes into ice
EvaporationLiquid → GasAdd heatWater boils into steam
CondensationGas → LiquidRemove heatSteam condenses on a cold surface
SublimationSolid → GasAdd heatDry ice turns directly to gas
DepositionGas → SolidRemove heatFrost forms on a cold window

The Water Cycle (Example of State Changes)

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ ☀️ Sun │ │ │ │ │ ▼ │ │ Evaporation (Liquid → Gas) │ │ │ │ │ ▼ │ │ Water vapor rises │ │ │ │ │ ▼ │ │ Condensation (Gas → Liquid) │ │ │ │ │ ▼ │ │ Clouds (water droplets) │ │ │ │ │ ▼ │ │ Precipitation (Rain, snow) │ │ │ │ │ ▼ │ │ Water returns to Earth │ │ │ │ │ └───────────────────────────────────────┘ │ ▲ │ │ │ │ │ Collection (rivers, lakes, oceans)│ │ │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Physical and Chemical Changes

Physical Changes

FeatureExplanation
No new substance is formedSame material before and after
Usually reversibleCan be changed back
ExamplesMelting ice, breaking glass, dissolving sugar

Chemical Changes

FeatureExplanation
A new substance is formedDifferent material after change
Usually irreversibleCannot be changed back easily
ExamplesBurning wood, cooking food, rusting iron

Comparison Table

Physical ChangeChemical Change
No new substanceNew substance formed
ReversibleUsually irreversible
Example: Ice → WaterExample: Wood → Ash

Properties of Materials

Materials have different properties that make them useful for different purposes.

PropertyMeaningExample
HardnessResistance to scratchingDiamond is very hard
SoftnessEasy to change shapeClay, rubber
FlexibilityCan bend without breakingRubber band, metal wire
BrittlenessBreaks easilyGlass, chalk
ConductivityAllows heat/electricity to passCopper, iron
InsulationDoes NOT allow heat/electricity to passPlastic, wood, rubber
TransparencyAllows light to passGlass, clear plastic
TranslucencyAllows some light to passFrosted glass
OpacityDoes NOT allow light to passWood, metal, brick

Measuring Matter

MeasurementWhat it measuresToolUnit
MassAmount of matterWeighing scalegram (g), kilogram (kg)
Volume (liquids)Space taken upMeasuring cylindermilliliter (mL), liter (L)
Volume (regular solids)Length × Width × HeightRulercubic cm (cm³)
Volume (irregular solids)Water displacementMeasuring cylindermL or cm³
DensityMass per volumeFormulag/cm³

💡 Density = Mass ÷ Volume

Try It Yourself!

Exercise 1: Fill in the Blanks

  1. Anything that has mass and takes up space is called ____________.
  2. The three states of matter are ____________, ____________, and ____________.
  3. Changing from solid to liquid is called ____________.
  4. Changing from liquid to gas is called ____________.
  5. ____________ change forms a new substance.

Exercise 2: Solid, Liquid, or Gas?

Write the state of matter for each.

  1. Ice ______
  2. Steam ______
  3. Milk ______
  4. Air ______
  5. Rock ______
  6. Oxygen ______

Exercise 3: Melting, Freezing, or Evaporation?

Identify the change of state.

  1. Water turning into ice → ____________
  2. Ice turning into water → ____________
  3. Water turning into steam → ____________
  4. Steam turning into water on a cold lid → ____________

Exercise 4: Physical or Chemical Change?

Write “Physical” or “Chemical”.

  1. Cutting paper ______
  2. Burning a matchstick ______
  3. Melting chocolate ______
  4. Rusting of iron ______
  5. Dissolving salt in water ______

Exercise 5: Match the Property

PropertyExample
Hardnessa) Rubber band stretches
Flexibilityb) Diamond cannot be scratched
Brittlenessc) Glass breaks easily
Transparencyd) We can see through glass
Opacitye) Wood does not allow light to pass

Answer Key

Exercise 1: 1. matter, 2. solid, liquid, gas; 3. melting, 4. evaporation, 5. Chemical

Exercise 2: 1. Solid, 2. Gas, 3. Liquid, 4. Gas, 5. Solid, 6. Gas

Exercise 3: 1. Freezing, 2. Melting, 3. Evaporation, 4. Condensation

Exercise 4: 1. Physical, 2. Chemical, 3. Physical, 4. Chemical, 5. Physical

Exercise 5: Hardness→b, Flexibility→a, Brittleness→c, Transparency→d, Opacity→e

Great work! You now understand matter – the stuff that makes up everything in the universe!
